12.1: Arjuna asks which path leads more directly to realization: devotion directed toward the guiding presence within existence, or contemplation of the imperishable and unmanifest reality.
12.2: Krishna said: Those who steady their minds on the living truth behind existence, remaining devoted with deep trust, are considered deeply united with the path.
12.3: Others contemplate the imperishable, the unseen and formless reality that pervades everything.
12.4: Through discipline of the senses, balance of mind, and dedication to the welfare of all beings, they too reach the same truth.
12.5: Yet the path of the formless and unseen is difficult for those living in embodied life.
12.6: But those who dedicate their actions to the highest truth and hold it as their deepest aim, meditating with wholehearted devotion,
12.7: are lifted beyond the cycle of fear and suffering because their minds remain centered on the deeper reality.
12.8: Fix your mind on the deeper reality behind existence and let your understanding rest in it. In doing so you live in harmony with it.
12.9: If you cannot hold the mind there steadily, then approach it through repeated and sincere practice.
12.10: If practice is difficult, then perform your actions in the spirit of service to the highest truth. Through such action growth unfolds.
12.11: If even this feels difficult, then simply let go of attachment to the results of actions and act with inner discipline.
12.12: Understanding is better than mechanical practice. Meditation is deeper than understanding. But freedom from attachment to results brings immediate peace.
12.13: One who holds no hatred toward any being, who is compassionate and kind, free from possessiveness and ego, steady in pleasure and pain, and forgiving,
12.14: content, disciplined, self-controlled, and firm in purpose, with mind and understanding dedicated to truth, lives deeply aligned with the path.
12.15: One who does not disturb the world and is not disturbed by it, who is free from envy, fear, and agitation, lives in harmony with reality.
12.16: One who expects little, lives with clarity and purity, acts skillfully and impartially, and releases unnecessary pursuits walks this path well.
12.17: One who neither clings to joy nor resists difficulty, who lets go of desire and fear, and remains devoted to truth lives deeply aligned with this way of life.
12.18: One who remains steady toward friend and enemy, honor and dishonor, pleasure and pain, free from attachment,
12.19: who treats praise and criticism alike, lives simply, remains steady in mind, and stays devoted to truth walks in harmony with the deeper reality.
12.20: Those who follow this way of living truthfully with trust and sincerity, holding the highest truth as their aim, walk a deeply valued path.
13.1: Arjuna asks about the nature of existence: the difference between nature and spirit, the body and the awareness that knows it, knowledge and what is truly worth knowing.
13.2: Krishna said: The body is called the field, and the one who is aware of the body and its experiences is called the knower of the field.
13.3: The same awareness exists within every field of experience. Understanding both the field and the knower is true knowledge.
13.4: Now listen as I briefly explain the nature of the field, its origins, its changes, and the power of the knower within it.
13.5: These truths have long been described by sages and preserved in the teachings and insights of ancient wisdom traditions.
13.6: The field includes the elements of nature, the mind, the senses, and the experiences of desire, pleasure, pain, and awareness itself.
13.7: True knowledge begins with humility, honesty, nonviolence, patience, sincerity, respect for learning, purity, and self-discipline.
13.8: It includes freedom from ego, detachment from sensory obsession, and thoughtful reflection on the realities of aging, illness, and death.
13.9: It also includes nonattachment to possessions and relationships, and steadiness in both favorable and unfavorable situations.
13.10: It includes unwavering dedication to truth, love of quiet reflection, and distance from restless distraction.
13.11: A sincere search for understanding the soul and the goal of life is called knowledge. Anything that leads away from this is ignorance.
13.12: Now I will describe the truth worth knowing. Knowing it leads one to realize the eternal reality called Brahman.
13.13: This reality exists everywhere, perceiving through every being and extending throughout existence.
13.14: It expresses itself through the senses yet is not limited by them. It supports everything while remaining unattached.
13.15: It exists both within and beyond all beings, subtle and difficult to perceive, both distant and very near.
13.16: Though appearing divided among countless forms of life, it remains one and undivided.
13.17: It is the light behind all lights, beyond darkness, the source of knowledge and the goal of knowledge, dwelling in the heart of every being.
13.18: Understanding the body, knowledge, and this deeper reality leads a person into harmony with existence.
13.19: Both nature and the conscious self are without beginning. The changes we observe arise from nature.
13.20: Nature produces the structures and processes of life, while the conscious self experiences pleasure and pain.
13.21: When awareness becomes attached to the qualities of nature, it becomes bound to cycles of experience and rebirth.
13.22: Yet within the body exists a deeper presence that observes, supports, and sustains life.
13.23: One who understands the relationship between nature, the self, and the qualities of experience becomes free from rebirth.
13.24: Some perceive the soul through meditation. Others through understanding. Others through disciplined action.
13.25: Even those who learn through sincere listening can move beyond fear and mortality.
13.26: Every living being arises through the interaction of the field and the awareness that experiences it.
13.27: One who sees the same deeper presence existing equally in all beings truly sees.
13.28: Seeing this unity, such a person does not harm themselves or others and reaches the highest realization.
13.29: One who sees that nature performs all actions while the soul simply witnesses them sees clearly.
13.30: When one recognizes that all life arises from a single underlying reality, one realizes Brahman.
13.31: Because the soul is beginningless and beyond qualities, it remains untouched by the actions of the body.
13.32: Just as space is not stained by what happens within it, the soul remains unaffected by the events of life.
13.33: Just as the sun illuminates the world, awareness illuminates the field of experience.
13.34: Those who understand the difference between the body and the knower within it, and who see how liberation arises from this understanding, reach the highest realization.
